Neural Architecture Search for Robotics

This paper presents a novel approach to neural architecture search (NAS) specifically designed for robotic control systems. Traditional NAS methods focus on image classification tasks, but robotic applications require architectures that can process multi-modal sensor inputs and produce continuous control outputs in real-time.

We introduce RoboNAS, a hardware-aware search algorithm that considers the computational constraints of embedded systems commonly used in robotics. Our method explores architectures that balance accuracy with inference latency, ensuring that the discovered networks can run within the strict timing requirements of control loops.

The search space includes convolutional layers for visual processing, recurrent layers for temporal dependencies, and attention mechanisms for sensor fusion. We demonstrate that RoboNAS discovers architectures that outperform hand-designed baselines on three benchmarks: indoor navigation, manipulation tasks, and multi-robot coordination.

Our experimental evaluation shows that RoboNAS-discovered architectures achieve 15% higher success rates on navigation tasks while requiring 40% fewer floating-point operations compared to ResNet-based baselines. For manipulation tasks, the discovered architectures reduce grasp failure rates by 22% and improve cycle times by 18%.

We also analyze the discovered architectures to understand what structural patterns emerge when optimizing for robotic control. Interestingly, the search consistently discovers architectures with skip connections between sensor fusion layers and output heads, suggesting that direct sensor-to-action pathways are important for reactive control behaviors.

The implications of this work extend beyond the specific benchmarks studied. By automating architecture design for robotics, we reduce the expertise barrier for deploying deep learning in robotic systems. We release our search framework and pre-trained architectures as an open-source toolkit to accelerate research in this area.
